name: Coleman-Weinberg radiative symmetry breaking
summary: The Higgs (or analogous scalar) has a vanishing tree-level mass parameter; electroweak symmetry breaking is driven entirely by radiative corrections to the effective potential, generating a mass scale via dimensional transmutation. In the classically scale-invariant variant, the absence of a mass term is protected by classical conformal symmetry, removing the quadratic-divergence form of the hierarchy problem (Bardeen's argument).

addresses_puzzles.note: If one takes Bardeen's argument seriously, the absence of a fundamental mass term in a classically conformal SM means quadratic divergences are an artefact of the cutoff regulator; the surviving naturalness question is the multiplicative running of dimensionless couplings. Embedding requires extra scalars or a richer symmetry-breaking sector since pure SM Coleman-Weinberg is excluded by m_H.

touches_benchmarks.note: Classically scale-invariant extensions typically need additional scalars to drive symmetry breaking; their mixing with the Higgs predicts O(few %) coupling deviations within HL-LHC reach.
Strong first-order EW phase transition in many CW-extended models predicts a stochastic GW background at LISA-relevant frequencies.

new_fields: additional scalar(s) to drive radiative breaking (singlet, U(1)_B-L scalar, etc.)
new_scales: dimensional-transmutation scale ~ EW (set by RG running)
introduces.note: New scalar masses and quartic couplings; the EW scale itself is no longer a free parameter but generated dynamically.

history: Coleman and Weinberg (1973) 'Radiative Corrections as the Origin of Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking', Phys. Rev. D 7, 1888 — introduced the mechanism for scalar QED. Bardeen (1995, FERMILAB-CONF-95-391-T) argued that classical scale invariance softens the hierarchy problem in the SM. Modern revivals (Foot, Kobakhidze, McDonald, Volkas; Iso-Okada-Orikasa) study scalar extensions that realise EW breaking radiatively.

references.citation: Coleman and Weinberg, 'Radiative Corrections as the Origin of Spontaneous Symmetry Breaking', Phys. Rev. D 7, 1888 (1973)
Bardeen, 'On Naturalness in the Standard Model', FERMILAB-CONF-95-391-T (1995)
